summary: Christopher Manning reflects on the unexpected triumph of natural language processing and LLMs over formal reasoning systems in AI. He argues that while current LLMs possess vast knowledge, true intelligence requires the ability to learn and adapt in an ever-varying world through interaction. He also highlights his recent research on improving neural architectures via universal transformers and developing linguistic agents that learn by interacting with web environments. Christopher Manning discusses the limitations of current static LLMs in acting as reliable web agents. He proposes a framework for unsupervised, interactive learning where agents explore websites via accessibility trees to discover goals and trajectories, ultimately fine-tuning smaller, highly capable models. He also argues that AI should complement rather than replicate human intelligence and that transformers naturally learn grammar without explicit human-built rules.

principles.statement: Human language is the ultimate knowledge representation.
principles.rationale: For decades, the AI field believed it needed formal representations, knowledge graphs, and formal reasoning systems to achieve intelligence. However, treating human language text directly as the knowledge encoding has proven to be the successful path.
principles.source_quote: what we've ended up with is human language as the knowledge representation and human language thinking as how we do our reasoning.

principles.statement: True intelligence is the ability to learn and adapt in an ever-varying world.
principles.rationale: Current LLMs have enormous knowledge and can interpolate across what they've seen, but they struggle with unseen circumstances. Intelligence fundamentally requires learning to cope with new, unexpected conditions.
principles.source_quote: the ability to learn and perform a range of techniques to solve problems and achieve goals appropriate to the context in an uncertain ever varying world.

principles.statement: The LLM revolution required a trinity of compute, model flexibility, and data.
principles.rationale: Large datasets existed earlier (e.g., Google's 2007 two-trillion word corpus), but early models lacked flexibility (n-grams). It wasn't until the transformer architecture provided scalable model flexibility, combined with massive compute and data, that LLMs took off.
principles.source_quote: it's really this complete trinity of compute model flexibility and data that was necessary

principles.statement: We must continue researching better neural architectures, not just scale data and compute.
principles.rationale: While the current industry playbook of throwing compute and data at models works well, the initial breakthrough relied on a powerful architecture (the transformer). Further progress, like systematic generalization, requires architectural innovations.
principles.source_quote: I think we also still need continued research on better neural architectures.

principles.statement: AI agents must learn from interacting with the world, not just from static pre-training.
principles.rationale: Current LLMs have built-in knowledge from pre-training and post-training, but they lack the ability to learn dynamically from interactions, making them brittle when navigating unfamiliar environments like the long tail of the web.
principles.source_quote: crucially, we don't have the ability to learn from interactions in the world. And so we're going to try and do that by learning inter interactively to build trajectories.

principles.statement: Large neural networks learn human grammar on their own; explicitly building grammars for them is a dead end.
principles.rationale: Formalisms like context-free grammars are idealizations that leave out a lot of nuance. Transformers naturally learn grammatical structures and broader linguistic connections directly from data.
principles.source_quote: I suspect that there isn't future in humans explicitly building grammarss and hoping that those can help transformers. I mean, I think instead the right way to think about it is that large neural networks like transformers are actually learning human language grammar by themselves.

principles.statement: Artificial intelligence does not need to exactly replicate human intelligence.
principles.rationale: AI has different capabilities, such as a massive breadth of knowledge that exceeds humans. The goal of AI should be to complement human strengths rather than duplicate human cognition exactly.
principles.source_quote: there's absolutely no reason why an artificial intelligence has to be trying to be exactly the same as human intelligence... we'd be much better aiming at complenting what humans are good at in our artificial intelligences.

frameworks.name: Interactive Linguistic Web Agent Loop
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building AI agents that need to navigate and perform tasks on the web (e.g., finding directions, filing issues).
steps: Define the agent's action space (e.g., typing, moving the mouse).
Provide the agent with an explicit objective or user instruction.
Supply the agent with a history of past events and actions.
Provide the current state of the environment using a textual accessibility tree rather than raw pixels.
Allow the agent to learn interactively by building trajectories in the environment.
frameworks.source_quote: We're going to give it an action space... give it an objective... tell it a history of what's happened in the past and then we'll tell it about its current state... the accessibility tree that corresponds to a web page.

frameworks.name: Layer-Grouped Universal Transformer
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing transformer architectures to achieve better systematic generalization and parameter efficiency.
steps: Replace standard MLPs with a mixture of experts using individual sigmoids to prevent expert competition.
Extend the mixture of experts down to the attention layer (OMV projections) to reduce the number of heads.
Apply 'per-layer norm' (removing layer norm on the residual stream) to prevent the residual stream from growing continuously.
Group 2 to 3 layers together to implement complex circuits, and repeat this group recurrently across the depth of the network.
frameworks.source_quote: we could get a lot further in uni using universal transformers if instead we had a group of layers which could implement circuits or universal computation and then we can repeat that group of layers recurrently going up.

frameworks.name: Unsupervised Open-Ended Goal Discovery
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building autonomous web agents that need to navigate and operate on new, unknown websites without prior human demonstration data.
steps: Provide a base LLM with the textual accessibility tree of a web page.
Prompt the LLM to interact with the page and explore what can be done.
Follow the exploration trajectory for a few steps.
Use the LLM to propose a goal that the current trajectory is heading toward.
Evaluate if the trajectory is making progress toward the goal; keep it if yes, prune if it is just spinning its wheels.
Use rejection sampling to generate better, optimized trajectories for the discovered goals.
Fine-tune a smaller, faster model on the collected interaction data.
frameworks.source_quote: we are able to learn by unsupervised interaction from any site that you show. So, it tries to discover what you can do with a website. It then works out ways of satisfying goals and that can then build a strong system for a web agent.

mental_models.name: The Landmine Test
mental_models.description: A heuristic lens for evaluating definitions of Artificial Intelligence. If a proposed definition of AI perfectly describes a simple, non-intelligent mechanical device (like a landmine), the definition is too broad and fundamentally flawed.
mental_models.example: Manning applies this to the California legislature's definition of AI (AB2885). He notes that a landmine is autonomous, has an explicit objective, infers from input (being stepped on), and generates outputs that influence the physical environment—yet no one would call a landmine intelligent.

mental_models.name: The Scientist in the Crib
mental_models.description: A metaphor for true intelligence, based on how human children learn. Intelligence is developed by actively interacting with the world, playing around, and inferring causality to understand how things work, rather than just passively absorbing static data.
mental_models.example: Manning uses the example of little kids kicking over blocks and making a mess of the household. They aren't just being destructive; they are playing around to discover how the physical world works so they can deal with different environments.

mental_models.name: The Long Tail of the Web
mental_models.description: The internet consists of a few massive, well-known sites and an enormous number of niche, obscure sites. AI agents cannot rely solely on pre-trained knowledge because they will inevitably encounter this long tail where they lack prior context and must learn by poking around.
mental_models.example: GPT-4 might know how to navigate Amazon, but it won't know how to find a gasket on a niche site like 'espresso parts' without interactive exploration.

heuristics: Throw compute, data, and money at things and you can get a long way.
To survive, agents must have enough built-in knowledge to survive and the ability to learn from interaction.
To solve latency in web agents, use smaller models (like 8B) that can eventually run on-device rather than round-tripping to a server.

signature_quotes.text: what we've ended up with is human language as the knowledge representation and human language thinking as how we do our reasoning.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the philosophical shift away from formal knowledge graphs toward text-based LLMs.

signature_quotes.text: the ability to learn and perform a range of techniques to solve problems and achieve goals appropriate to the context in an uncertain ever varying world.
signature_quotes.context: Manning's own definition of intelligence, written for the Stanford HAI institute.

signature_quotes.text: this informal ubiquitous probably 100,000 years old technology invented by humans of human languages has completely won over the formal languages of recent centuries
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the surprising victory of NLP over formal logic in AI.

signature_quotes.text: By and large, they are good because they have enormous knowledge... but they're not actually very good at thinking about different circumstances that haven't been seen before.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why current LLMs lack true intelligence despite their impressive capabilities.

signature_quotes.text: crucially, we don't have the ability to learn from interactions in the world. And so we're going to try and do that by learning inter interactively to build trajectories.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the missing piece in the current LLM training stack.

signature_quotes.text: I suspect that there isn't future in humans explicitly building grammarss and hoping that those can help transformers.
signature_quotes.context: Answering a question about whether decades of linguistic grammar research can augment LLMs.

signature_quotes.text: there's absolutely no reason why an artificial intelligence has to be trying to be exactly the same as human intelligence.
signature_quotes.context: Responding to a question about whether AI needs to replicate human survival-based intelligence.

anti_patterns.description: Confusing vast knowledge with actual intelligence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Memorizing massive amounts of information (like a tax lawyer or Wikipedia) allows a system to interpolate across what it has seen, but it does not equip the system to adapt, learn, or infer causality in new, unseen circumstances.

anti_patterns.description: Defining Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) purely as the ability to perform economically valuable tasks.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Most human work is routine and repetitive. Automating white-collar work is economically valuable, but it drastically understates true human intelligence, which involves adapting to an ever-varying world.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on formal reasoning languages and knowledge graphs to achieve AI.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Historically, AI assumed it needed formal representations. However, human language text itself proved to be a far more effective, scalable, and natural knowledge encoding.

anti_patterns.description: Using pre-layer norm in universal transformers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: In pre-layer norm transformers, the residual stream grows continuously over layers. If you try to share weights across layers (as in a universal transformer), the weights will be at the wrong scale—too big for low layers and too small for high layers.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on behavioral cloning (human activity traces) to train web agents.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is costly and cannot scale to the huge, constantly growing number of websites in the long tail. Agents need to learn to poke around new sites autonomously.

anti_patterns.description: Explicitly building formal grammars to help train or improve transformers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Formal grammars (like context-free grammars) are idealizations that miss a lot of nuance, whereas large neural networks naturally learn grammar and broader linguistic connections on their own.

anti_patterns.description: Relying entirely on vision language models for current web agents.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Despite the appeal of processing raw pixels, current vision language models don't work very well in practice compared to using text-based accessibility trees.
